results.possibleIdentity: Robert Bruce Newman (1935–2018) was an American poet, artist, art critic, and gallery director active in the New York art scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. He founded and directed Gain Ground, an influential alternative gallery space at 246 West 80th Street in Manhattan, which operated from 1969 to 1970 and served as a forum for concrete poetry and technological art. Newman was a key figure in the concrete poetry movement and an art critic for publications such as Arts Magazine and the 57th Street Review. His artistic work frequently bridged the gap between language and visual art, often incorporating advanced technology. One of his most notable works, 'Power Throne' (1969), was a multi-sensory electronic environment that utilized a heart-sound feedback system to immerse participants in the sound of their own heartbeats. He was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) community; his project 'Liberty Light' (1970), which proposed a new laser-based lighting system for the Statue of Liberty, is documented within E.A.T.-related archives. In 1970, he became a student of the Tibetan meditation master Chögyam Trungpa Rinpoche and later focused on Tibetan Buddhist art and iconography while continuing to explore 'visionwork'—a practice combining meditation with visual art.
